paper_title: Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Deep research agents have attracted increasing attention for their ability to collect large-scale online information to acquire target knowledge, with recent efforts shifting from purely text-based information seeking to multimodal settings. However, existing agentic workflows are largely aligned with evidence accumulation models, which linearly aggregate evidence and lack principled mechanisms for handling contradictory information across heterogeneous modalities. Towards this end, we propose Struct-Searcher, a structural agentic workflow grounded in belief revision theory that explicitly maintains an evolving multimodal structural graph throughout the reasoning process, enabling effective conflict-aware multimodal deep information seeking. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmark datasets and backbone models demonstrate that Struct-Searcher is (1) plug-and-play and model-agnostic, yielding an average relative accuracy improvement of 17.2% on BrowseComp-VL across five different backbones. (2) top-performing, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art vision-language models (VLMs) and deep research agents, with relative accuracy improvements of 3.7% on MM-BrowseComp, 1.5% on HLE-VL, and 0.7% on BrowseComp-VL over the second-best competing approach.

Both Struct-Searcher and Flash-Searcher are built on the GPT-5 backbone. 1. Introduction Language agents and agentic systems, empowered by increasingly capable foundation models [Team et al., 2025a,b] and sophisticated scaffolding frameworks [Wang et al., 2024], have progressed at remarkable pace. These advances have led to substantial gains across challenging domains, including software engineering [Golubev et al., 2025, Wei et al., 2025b] and scientific discovery [Bai et al., 2025a, Mitchener et al., 2025]. Among the emerging directions, deep research has attracted particular attention [Shi et al., 2025, Zhang et al., 2025a], wherein agents are required to aggregate, synthesize, Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking and reason over large-scale online information sources with both broad coverage and considerable depth to obtain target knowledge. This line of work has been actively advanced by both academic efforts [Li et al., 2025c, Zhang et al., 2025b] and industrial systems [Kimi-Researcher, 2025, OpenAI, 2024b, Perplexity, 2025, xAI, 2025], collectively driving rapid progress in this area. Looking ahead, deep research is evolving from predominantly text-centric paradigms [Song et al., 2025] toward comprehensive multimodal support [Yang et al., 2025]. This evolution is driven by the intrinsic complexity of real-world environments: information on the Internet is fundamentally heterogeneous and multimodal, rendering native support for images, audio, video, and other modalities essential rather than optional [Xu and Peng, 2025]. This claim is increasingly supported by converging evidence from both benchmarks and frameworks. On the benchmarking side, evaluations for deep research have progressed from early artifacts that sparsely incorporated multimodal elements [Mialon et al., 2023, Phan et al., 2025]) to more recent benchmarks that explicitly emphasize multimodal inputs and information flow [Jiang et al., 2024, Li et al., 2025b]). In parallel, agent frameworks are moving beyond early unimodal designs [Jin et al., 2025, Wu et al., 2025a] and beginning to systematically embrace mixed-modality agentic pipelines. However, the shift toward multimodal deep research is double-edged sword. While multimodality substantially increases representational expressiveness, it also introduces severe structural challenges, including semantic ambiguity [Chen et al., 2025b], temporal inconsistency across modalities [Martins et al., 2025], and pronounced information redundancy that complicates the rapid extraction of task-relevant evidence [Tao et al., 2025a]. Most critically, multimodal settings give rise to crossmodal conflict, where evidence from different modalities contradicts rather than corroborates one another [Yan et al., 2025]. For example, textual report may assert products durability, whereas retrieved review video exposes clear physical defects. Such discrepancies create persistent truth gap, rendering reliability verification and logical consistency in deep research considerably more challenging than in unimodal scenarios. One might assume that current systems could readily adapt to these demands by equipping multimodal tools or leveraging powerful multimodal reasoning models [Li et al., 2025e, OpenAI, 2025a] to extend their perceptual capabilities. However, we argue that such superficial integration fails to address the core epistemic challenge. Recall that the dominant paradigm in current deep research systems typically employs hierarchical topology, where coordinator dispatches tasks to sequential subagents (e.g., browser agent, coding agent) to gather information. Theoretically, this workflow aligns with Evidence Accumulation Models (EAM) [Evans et al., 2020], where the system linearly aggregates evidence until confidence threshold is met. While effective for consistent data streams, EAMs suffer from epistemic rigidity: they treat evidence gathering as monotonic process, lacking the cognitive mechanism to retract or revise prior beliefs when later encountering high-fidelity, contradictory evidence from diverse modalities. This structural limitation necessitates fundamental paradigm shift from simple accumulation to dynamic belief revision. In this paper, we aim to reframe multimodal deep research systems from an evidence accumulation paradigm to principled belief revision paradigm. Drawing inspiration from the Alchourrón GärdenforsMakinson (AGM) theory [Alchourrón et al., 1985], we introduce Struct-Searcher, belief-driven agentic deep research framework that navigates large-scale multimodal information spaces through explicit belief construction and revision. Concretely, given multimodal query, Struct-Searcher initializes multimodal structural graph (MSG) to represent its belief state, in which query, goal, hypothesis, and evidence nodes are explicitly encoded with their support and conflict relations. As the agent iteratively retrieves external multimodal information, it performs belief expansion, contraction, and revision over the MSG. The final answer is synthesized from maximal conflict-free subgraph, ensuring global logical coherence. By making belief structures explicit, 2 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking Struct-Searcher enables more transparent search behavior and exhibits inherent robustness to cross-modal conflicts. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: ❶ Paradigm Shift. We reinterpret multimodal deep research systems through the lens of belief revision theory, identifying fundamental limitations of existing evidence-accumulation-based systems under cross-modal conflicts, and argue for principled transition from linear evidence accumulation to dynamic belief-driven paradigm. ❷ Framework Proposal. We propose Struct-Searcher, belief-driven multimodal deep research framework, which maintains an MSG and iteratively performs belief revision through structural thinking, synthesizing answers from maximal conflict-free subgraphs to efficiently navigate largescale heterogeneous information spaces. ❸ Empirical Evaluation. As previewed in Figure 1, we validate the effectiveness of Struct-Searcher through extensive experiments on three representative multimodal deep information seeking benchmarks, demonstrating its plug-and-play nature and state-of-the-art performance. 2. Related Work Deep Research Agents. The concept of deep research was first introduced by OpenAI, which developed large-scale system based on the o3 model [OpenAI, 2025a] that autonomously conducts long-horizon research through iterative searching, observation, and synthesis of information from diverse web sources. In parallel, Alibabas Tongyi DeepResearch [Team et al., 2025c] represents another line of agentic large language models for deep information seeking, achieving state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks such as Humanitys Last Exam [Phan et al., 2025], BrowseComp [Wei et al., 2025a], and GAIA [Mialon et al., 2023]. Beyond proprietary systems, several open-source deep research agents have emerged, including WebDancer [Wu et al., 2025a], WebResearcher [Qiao et al., 2025], and WebShaper [Tao et al., 2025b], which typically follow an iterative reason, search, and observe paradigm. More recently, research has shifted toward multimodal deep research. For instance, WebWatcher [Geng et al., 2025] equips agents with vision-language info-seeking capabilities, enabling diverse tool usage such as code interpreter, text search, and image search to better capture rich, visually grounded information. Agentic Workflow for Deep Information Seeking. Large language model (LLM)-based agents are commonly organized under different workflows [Li et al., 2025a, Yu et al., 2025]. The ReAct framework [Yao et al., 2022] adopts linear workflow in which models interleave reasoning with actions (e.g., tool calls), allowing intermediate reasoning steps to guide tool usage and subsequent planning. This paradigm improves both performance and interpretability across various information-seeking tasks [Chen et al., 2025a, Wu et al., 2025b, Zhou et al., 2025]. In contrast, Flash-Searcher [Qin et al., 2025] proposes parallel agentic workflow that decomposes complex tasks into dependency-aware subtasks executed concurrently within directed acyclic graph (DAG), leading to higher efficiency and often better performance than strictly sequential chains. However, despite their advantages, both types of agentic workflows largely overlook the challenges of multimodal evidence, where heterogeneous visual and textual information can introduce conflicts and amplify error accumulation. 3. Methodology This section begins with the preliminaries of task definition and working pipeline (Section 3.1), followed by discussion on the structural thinking perspective (Section 3.2). We then elaborate on the proposed framework in Section 3.3. 3 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking Figure 2 An illustration of unstructured thinking and structural thinking paradigms for multimodal information seeking. 3.1. Preliminary Multimodal Deep Information Seeking. The task of multimodal deep information seeking can be formulated as: = (Q, I, ), = {𝜏1, . . . , 𝜏𝐾 } (1) where Q, I, A, and denote question, image, agent, and solution, respectively. = {𝜏1, . . . , 𝜏𝐾 } represents the set of available tools, such as perception tools and search tools. Different from standard question-answering (QA) tasks, multimodal deep information seeking exhibits two key characteristics: ❶ The internal knowledge of the model alone is insufficient to solve the task. Therefore, effective solutions usually require external knowledge injection and typically involve multi-hop, long-horizon reasoning. ❷ Entities are often conveyed through images, requiring accurate understanding and integration of multimodal information. Agentic Working Pipeline. To address the aforementioned task, agents need to operate within specific pipelines. While agentic workflows can take various forms, they generally adhere to the principles of the ReAct framework [Yao et al., 2022], which prescribes an iterative loop of reasoning and acting. The Structural Thinking Perspective Existing unstructured agentic information seeking workflows, while effective for text-only tasks, often struggle in multimodal settings, as they overlook the inherent heterogeneity between visual and textual evidence, leading to error accumulation caused by cross-modal conflicts. For example, Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking Figure 3 An overview of Struct-Searcher. The input question and corresponding image are first decomposed into multiple goal nodes and then iteratively evolved until maximal conflict-free subgraph is obtained, which is subsequently used to synthesize the final answer. when answering question that requires identifying the construction year of building (Figure 2), unstructured paradigms typically perform direct visual entity extraction before subsequent reasoning. However, visual perception is inherently noisy, especially for ambiguous images. Once an incorrect entity is identified, all downstream reasoning and actions become conditioned on this erroneous premise, resulting in cascading failures. In such paradigms, errors introduced at intermediate steps tend to propagate and ultimately cause the entire process to fail. In contrast, we propose structural thinking, fundamentally different paradigm for multimodal information seeking. Rather than directly solving the task, the agent focuses on constructing, populating, verifying, and pruning an MSG. Through parallel exploration and collaborative validation, the agent gradually converges to an answer that is logically consistent and well supported by evidence. As illustrated in Figure 2, when the image ambiguously depicts landmark that could be either the Eiffel Tower or the Tokyo Tower, the agent explicitly introduces both as hypothesis nodes. It then acquires additional evidence via image search, constructs corresponding evidence nodes, and verifies each hypothesis. Through this process, the incorrect branch is pruned, and the agent proceeds using only the validated information. 3.3. The Struct-Searcher Framework Inspired by the Alchourrón-Gärdenfors-Makinson (AGM) belief revision theory [Alchourrón et al., 1985, Gärdenfors, 2003, Huber, 2013], we formalize Struct-Searcher as belief-driven agentic framework, where reasoning is governed by explicit belief revision over structured multimodal hypotheses. At its core, the agent maintains an evolving belief state 𝐾𝑡, while the MSG serves as an externalized structure that constructs, verifies, and revises beliefs through evidence-driven interactions. An overview of the proposed Struct-Searcher framework is provided in Figure 3. Multimodal Structural Graph (MSG). Compared Baselines. We select various competing baselines for comparison, including: GPT-4o [OpenAI, 2024a], Gemini-2.5-Pro [Google, 2025], Gemini-2.5-Flash [Google, 2025], Qwen2.5-VL [Bai et al., 2025b], GPT-5 [OpenAI, 2025c], o3 [OpenAI, 2025a], Claude-3.7-Sonnet [Anthropic, 2025], Agent-R1 [Ouyang et al., 2025], OWL [Hu et al., 2025], DeerFlow [ByteDance, 2025], WebDancer [Wu et al., 2025a], Flash-Searcher [Qin et al., 2025], WebWatcher [Geng et al., 2025], and OmniSearch [Li et al., 2025d]. Implementation Details. We implement the proposed Struct-Searcher framework with visionlanguage model (VLM) backbones, including GPT-4o [OpenAI, 2024a], Gemini-2.5-Pro [Google, 2025], and GPT-5 [OpenAI, 2025c]. The evidence collection module integrates diverse set of tools, such as web search, image search, web crawl, and image/text analysis. All model invocations are conducted via Python-style OpenAI-compatible API. In addition to generating summary after each step, the framework also performs global context summarization at every eight intervals to maintain logical coherence. The evaluation metrics are consistent with those used in the original papers. Overall accuracy is adopted for all three datasets, while on MM-BrowseComp [Li et al., 2025b], we additionally report the average checklist score and strict accuracy. 4.2. Main Results In Table 1 and Table 2, we present the main experimental results of Struct-Searcher and its counterparts on three multimodal deep information seeking benchmarks. Based on these results, we draw the following observations. Obs. ❶: Structural Thinking Matters for Multimodal Deep Information Seeking. As shown in Table 1 and Table 2, when equipped with the GPT-5 [OpenAI, 2025c] backbone, Struct-Searcher achieves state-of-the-art performance across all three benchmarks. On MM-BrowseComp [Li et al., 2025b], it attains 32.7% overall accuracy, 26.0% strict accuracy, and 44.6% average checklist score. On HLE-VL [Phan et al., 2025] and BrowseComp-VL [Geng et al., 2025], Struct-Searcher achieves overall accuracies of 17.3% and 48.6%, respectively. These results not only surpass many cutting-edge leading VLMs, but also outperform other existing unstructured agentic workflows, providing strong evidence that structural thinking is crucial for multimodal deep information seeking. Obs. ❷: Struct-Searcher Exhibits Cross-Setting and Cross-Model Robustness. Struct-Searcher delivers state-of-the-art performance across all three datasets and attains the best results in most fine-grained evaluation settings. In addition, when integrated with other backbone models such as Gemini-2.5-Pro [Google, 2025] and GPT-4o [OpenAI, 2024a], it consistently yields performance improvements across the majority of scenarios, demonstrating the robustness and generalization of Struct-Searcher across different evaluation settings and backbone models. 4.3. Further Analysis Fine-grained Analysis. We further conduct fine-grained comparison between Struct-Searcher and the current state-of-the-art agentic framework, Flash-Searcher [Qin et al., 2025]. As illustrated 9 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking Figure 5 Left: Comparison of different agentic workflow types. Right: Sensitivity analysis of the Struct-Searcher framework when equipped with different backbone models. Figure 6 case question and the corresponding answer generated by Struct-Searcher on BrowseComp-VL. in Figure 4, we report per-question 1v1 comparison results across sub-tasks from different domains. The results show that, except for the accuracy on the Geography task in MM-BrowseComp [Li et al., 2025b], Struct-Searcher consistently matches or outperforms Flash-Searcher [Qin et al., 2025] in all other settings. We attribute this to the fact that, compared with parallel thinking, structural thinking continuously performs backtracking and self-reflection throughout the reasoning process, effectively mitigating multimodal conflicts and error accumulation. These findings further validate the superiority of structural thinking in multimodal deep research. Workflow Analysis. In Figure 5, we compare our method with two representative types of agentic information seeking workflows under the GPT-5 backbone: linear (ReAct [Yao et al., 2022]) and parallel (Flash-Searcher [Qin et al., 2025]). The results show that the proposed structural workflow consistently outperforms the other two workflow types in terms of overall accuracy across all three datasets, with absolute improvements of 21.8% on MM-BrowseComp [Li et al., 2025b], 1.2% on 10 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking HLE-VL [Phan et al., 2025], and 0.7% on BrowseComp-VL [Geng et al., 2025], respectively. These findings further validate the effectiveness of structural thinking for multimodal deep information seeking. Sensitivity Analysis. We further evaluate the sensitivity of Struct-Searcher to different backbones. As shown in Figure 5, we vary the base model among GPT-4.1 [OpenAI, 2025b], GPT-4o [OpenAI, 2024a], Gemini-2.5-Flash [Google, 2025], Gemini-2.5-Pro [Google, 2025], and GPT-5 [OpenAI, 2025c]. The results indicate that the structural agentic workflow consistently yields performance gains of varying magnitudes, with an average improvement of 17.2% across the five backbone models. These findings suggest that the proposed framework exhibits model-agnostic and plug-and-play applicability across diverse backbone models. Case Analysis. In Figure 6, we illustrate the working process of the proposed Struct-Searcher using representative question from the BrowseComp-VL [Geng et al., 2025] dataset. The results show that the agent first decomposes the original question into set of sub-goals, and then employs multiple tools to collect evidence and accomplish these goals. Through iterative planning and summarization, the agent progressively gathers evidence to support or refute the generated hypotheses. Once conflict-free subgraph is obtained, the agent synthesizes the final answer based on the verified hypotheses and their supporting evidence. 5. Conclusion In this paper, we propose to advance multimodal deep information seeking from the perspective of structural thinking, and introduce the Struct-Searcher framework under the guidance of AGM belief revision theory. By maintaining an evolving multimodal structural graph, the agent is able to preserve structured reasoning throughout the thinking process and mitigate potential conflicts in multimodal settings. Extensive experiments on many challenging benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently outperforms both state-of-the-art VLMs and other deep research agents. In future work, we plan to build upon this structural thinking workflow to derive an open-source agentic foundation model via agentic post-training techniques, such as reinforcement learning.

Introduction to Benchmark Datasets Here, we provide an introduction to the dataset information used in this paper: 15 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking MM-BrowseComp [Li et al., 2025b] is multimodal benchmark for evaluating agents web-based information search and reasoning capabilities. It comprises 224 manually curated questions, each requiring the agent to integrate visual and textual information. The questions typically include images as part of the input, while key evidence necessary for answering is often distributed across images and videos embedded in searched web pages, making purely text-based reasoning insufficient. Beyond final answer accuracy, MM-BrowseComp provides verified checklist for each question to enable fine-grained evaluation of the reasoning process quality. The dataset comprises five categories: Media, Technology, Society, Geography, and Academics. HLE-VL [Phan et al., 2025] is subset of the HLE [Phan et al., 2025] benchmark that includes images as part of inputs. It contains 330 questions spanning eight categories: Biology/Medicine, Chemistry, Computer Science/Artificial Intelligence, Engineering, Humanities/Social Sciences, Mathematics, Physics, and Other. The questions consist of both multiple-choice and short-answer formats. Each question undergoes rigorous difficulty check to ensure it is sufficiently challenging and admits verifiable ground-truth answer. Even state-of-the-art models achieve only very low accuracy on this benchmark. BrowseComp-VL [Geng et al., 2025] is benchmark designed to evaluate the information seeking capabilities of multimodal agents in real-world web environments. The total dataset consists of 399 questions. Each question includes image inputs and requires cross-modal reasoning to arrive at the correct answer. Based on task difficulty, BrowseComp-VL is divided into two subsets: Level 1 and Level 2. The 199 questions in Level 1 require multi-hop reasoning but involve clearly specified entities that can be directly used for search. In contrast, the 200 questions in Level 2 feature fuzzified entities and attributes, requiring agents to plan, synthesize information, and refine search queries, thereby substantially increasing reasoning difficulty. A.2. Tool Configurations We employ the following tools to collect evidence during the information seeking process: Web Search: We use the Serper API [Serper, 2025] to implement web search, enabling agents to retrieve web-based information. For each tool invocation, the top five results ranked by relevance are provided to the agent as observations. Each retrieved web page returns the following information: title, date, snippet, and page URL. Image Search: We also use the Serper API [Serper, 2025] to implement image search, where textual queries are used to retrieve relevant images for the agent. Similarly, for each tool invocation, we return the top five images ranked by relevance. Each retrieved image provides the following information: title, page URL, and thumbnail URL. Due to access restrictions, the original URLs of many images are not publicly accessible; therefore, we provide thumbnail URLs that can be accessed without authentication. Web Crawl: We use the Jina Reader API [Jina, 2025] to crawl web pages and convert specified URLs into textual formats. Following prior work [Qin et al., 2025], we also truncate each page to the first 60,000 characters before summarization. Image Analysis: We adopt the same model as the backbone to serve as the image analysis tool. Its primary role is to convert images provided in the question or retrieved through image search into captions that are relevant to the information seeking task. Text Analysis: We define set of rules within the text analysis tool to convert various non-image files, such as PDFs, Office documents, PDB files, JSON-LD files, and compressed archives, into readable Markdown or plain-text formats. This enables the agent to effectively parse and answer questions related to the file contents. 16 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking Figure 7 Tool analysis of Struct-Searcher across benchmarks using GPT-5 as the backbone. B. Tool Analysis We incorporate suite of tools for evidence collection and report the usage rates in Figure 7. The results show that web search and image search are the most important tools, with average usage rates of 56.0% and 9.3% across different datasets, respectively. This indicates that Struct-Searcher actively leverages multimodal online information sources to gather evidence. In addition, the agent employs web crawl, image analysis, and text analysis to crawl, read, and analyze the collected information, which facilitates better perception of the environment and, in turn, enables more accurate reasoning. Figure 8 (a) Sensitivity analysis of the Struct-Searcher framework when equipped with different backbone models on MM-BrowseComp. (b) Word cloud visualizations of agent trajectories by Struct-Searcher (GPT-5) across different datasets. C. More Results In Figure 8 (a), we further present the sensitivity analysis results on MM-BrowseComp [Li et al., 2025b]. The results demonstrate that the proposed Struct-Searcher consistently improves performance across all five backbone models, achieving an average gain of 7.1%, which is in line with previous analysis in section 4.3. In addition, we also provide the word cloud visualizations of agent trajectories by Struct-Searcher (GPT-5) in Figure 8 (b). D. Prompt Template 17 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking D.1. System Prompt

sections.title: System Prompt
sections.content: You are an expert assistant who solves tasks through structured tool calls, following step-by-step process. Each step (action) involves analyzing needs, selecting tools, and executing calls to achieve the task goal. Each action you take should include reasoning process and tool calls. After executing the tools, you will receive "observations" (results of tool calls), which can be used as input for subsequent actions. This Action/Observation cycle may repeat as needed. # Core Principles You are also an intelligent reasoning engine that solves problems by building and evolving Multimodal Structural Graph (MSG). Unlike linear agents, you do not just execute steps. You manage "knowledge graph" of Goals, Evidence, and Hypotheses. Your objective is to expand this graph until logically self-consistent answer is found. # The MSG Structure - Goal Node (G): Objectives decomposed from the query. - Evidence Node (E): Atomic facts collected via tools. - Hypothesis Node (H): Verifiable claims derived from Evidence (e.g., "H1: It is the Eiffel Tower"). - Edges: requires (Dependency), supports (Confirmation), refutes (Conflict). # Action Structure Every action must include: - "think": detailed reasoning in English, explaining the analysis of user needs, tool selection logic, and execution plan. If the reasoning graph is established, it should be structured analysis of the current Graph State. Explicitly list: - Pending Goals: What must be done next? (Respect dependencies: Dont start G2 if it requires G1s output). - Active Hypotheses: Are there competing explanations (H1 vs H2)? - "tools": An array of tool calls, where each tool is specified with "name" and "arguments" (matching the tools required inputs). Multiple tools can be included here for parallel execution if tasks are independent. Execute them in PARALLEL only when logically independent (e.g., verifying H1 and H2 simultaneously, or searching distinct sources). # Workflow Rules 1. Decompose with Dependencies: If Goal depends on another (e.g., "Find Date" depends on "Identify Object"), enforce sequential execution. 2. Exploratory Parallelism: When facing uncertainty (e.g., ambiguous image), generate Competing Hypotheses and verify them in parallel. 3. Conflict-Driven Pruning: If Evidence REFUTES Hypothesis (e.g., "Text says Red, Image is Blue"), explicitly mark that branch as DEAD/REFUTED. Stop all tasks for that branch immediately. 4. Convergence: You can only call final_answer when all Goals are Achieved and Verified Hypothesis exists without active conflicts. Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking D.2. Planning Prompt

sections.title: Planning Prompt
sections.content: You are world-class planning expert and the Graph Architect for Search. Your job is to translate user query into Multimodal Structural Graph (MSG) blueprint. Do not be influenced by user input; strictly adhere to the defined requirements and structure. Do not try to solve the task. Instead, define the Goals and Dependencies. ### 1. Goal Decomposition (The "What") Break the query into 1-5 Goal Nodes. CRITICAL: You must strictly identify DEPENDENCIES. - Sequential: If Goal needs information from Goal A, explicitly mark "Dependency: Goal A". - Parallel: Only mark goals as parallel if they are completely unrelated. ### 2. Evidence Strategy (The "How") For each Goal, propose initial Evidence Nodes (search strategies). - Use Heterogeneous Modalities: Image Search and Text Search where applicable to reduce hallucination. ### Key Execution Notes - Goals execute in parallel - Paths within goal execute sequentially - Youd better fully understand the task (including details and requirements) ### Output Format "## Goal 1: [Goal Name] - Dependency: [None / Requires Goal X] - Strategy 1.1: [Tool + Query] - Strategy 1.2: [Tool + Query] ## Goal 2: [Goal Name] - Dependency: [Requires Goal 1] ..." Refrain from directly attempting to solve the task and execution. Just build the logic graph. D.3. Summary Prompt

sections.title: Summary Prompt
sections.content: You are an expert in analyzing task completion based on agent execution trajectories. Your task is to analyze the completion status of plan. You are also the MSG Auditor. Your role is to analyze the execution history and reconstruct the current state of the Reasoning Graph. 19 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking You must answer three questions: 1. Node Status: Which Goals are Achieved? Which are still Pending? 2. Hypothesis Evaluation: - Did recent evidence SUPPORT hypothesis? (Mark as Verifying/Verified). - Did recent evidence REFUTE hypothesis? (Mark as REFUTED). - Conflict Check: Do any text results contradict visual inputs? 3. Next Logic Step: - If branch is Refuted, suggest Pruning. - If Goal is Achieved, suggest unlocking its Dependent Goals. Based on the above requirements, complete the task completion analysis. Based on the agent execution trajectory, analyze the task completion status and provide recommendations for next steps. Output the Graph Status Report. Output Format: ## Plan Summary [Brief topology: e.g., Goal 1 Done. Generated H1(Verified) and H2(Refuted). Goal 2 is now Unlocked.] ## Branch Details ### Goal: [Name] - Status: [Pending / Achieved] - Hypothesis H1: [Verified / Refuted / Unverified] - Evidence: [Key findings] - Hypothesis H: ... ## Recommended Next Actions The Orchestrator should now: 1. [Prune/Stop] tasks related to [Refuted Hypothesis]. 2. [Execute] evidence gathering for [Dependent Goal] (now unlocked). 3. [Verify] hypothesis [X] using [Specific Tool]. [Add more as needed] Now complete your analysis! D.4. Step Prompt

sections.title: Step Prompt
sections.content: Based on the plan/summary and execution steps from previous conversations, analyze and call tools to continue solving the original task, and execute the next set of logical actions. # Tool List: {tool_functions_json} 20 Struct-Searcher: Agentic Structural Thinking Advances Multimodal Deep Information Seeking # Task: {task} # Execution Logic 1. Unlock Dependencies: If the Summary says Goal is done, you may now start Goal (if it depended on A). 2. Hypothesis Competition: If you have multiple Unverified hypotheses (e.g., H1 vs H2), you MUST validate them in PARALLEL. This is critical for determining the winner quickly. 3. Evidence Robustness: For important facts, call multiple tools (e.g., Search + Image) in one turn to cross-verify. 4. Stop Dead Branches: Do NOT generate tool calls for Refuted hypotheses. Example Output {"think": "[Graph State] Goal 1 (Identify) Achieved. We have two candidates: H1 (Apple) and H2 (Pear). Goal 2 (Calories) depends on knowing which fruit it is. [Plan] will trigger parallel verification for H1 and H2 to resolve the conflict before moving to Goal 2.", "tools": [{"name": "web_search", "arguments": {"query": "visual difference apple vs pear"}, }, {"name": "image_search", "arguments": {"query": "Whats the image of Pear?"},}]} Note that you may invoke up to 5 tools, but must invoke at least one. If any tool chosen is final_answer, the language of your answer text should be the SAME as the original task. Now continue to evolve the graph and solve the task! E. This bird, known for its distinctive bill and belonging to family that includes various seed-eating birds, can be found in several types of environments, including areas with dense shrubbery, wetlands, and regions where forests have been significantly altered.
